summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his laptop power consumption analysis, this time measuring his newer Dell XPS M1330 ultraportable using a Kill-a-Watt electricity monitor. He systematically tests power draw from various components including the LCD, hard drive, WiFi, CPU, GPU, DVD drive, and fan. The data reveals that CPU and GPU are by far the largest power consumers, with the CPU jumping from 20W idle to 63W under full load. Based on these measurements, Atwood provides practical advice for maximizing battery life, recommending Vista's Power Saver mode which throttles CPU to 50% as the most effective strategy.
key_points: The CPU is the biggest battery drain, consuming 43W more under full load than at idle - far exceeding any other component
GPU-intensive tasks like gaming are the second largest power drain, adding 20-35W to baseline consumption
Screen brightness has minimal impact on power consumption, only varying by 3W across all settings
WiFi uses relatively little power when idle but can add 7W during active bandwidth transfers
Vista's Power Saver mode with 50% CPU throttle is the most practical way to extend battery life without constantly monitoring usage
money_quotes: It's awfully handy to know how much power the stuff around your house is using.
The rules of laptop power consumption haven't fundamentally changed.
Keep a serious eye on your CPU usage; you desperately want to avoid CPU intensive programs and websites while on battery.
They'll kill your battery life far faster than anything else you can do with your laptop.
On a dual-core CPU, giving up one core is usually no big deal for most tasks.
key_insight: CPU usage is the dominant factor in laptop battery life, and enabling automatic CPU throttling through power management settings is more effective than manually monitoring and adjusting individual components.
